So, here we are, private practice. We'll get to a few things to show you. I've invited a couple of people to show you cool things, but I, of course, have some stuff for you first. I'm going to use, because I had it, I'm going to use some dental slides and examples. But I assure you, private practice is private practice is private practice. Patient, client, doesn't matter. The processes are all the same. Everybody's doing the same thing, getting a lead, getting somebody to an appointment, etc., etc. So, forgive the dental terminology in some places. But I promise you, all I would have done is just taken dentist out and put P slash C for patient, client, and we would have had exactly the same conversation. So, I got a little lazy about the slides, but I promise you, it makes no difference. So, I want to talk mostly about internal marketing, about referrals, about the growth of the practice from within. And none of this is really new, but we've gotten smarter and smarter and smarter about it over the years, and have arrived at a fairly sophisticated and universally successful solution answer to the problems of getting maximum referrals. So, I've also asked my guest to talk and show you things more about internal marketing than external. So, this really is an internal marketing approach. But I want to start with just a quick sort of review of why people don't refer or don't refer any abundance that we think they should refer in. So, one is that satisfied patients don't refer. People think that, and I said it earlier today, you know, that, gee, we've done a good job, we've healed the sick, we've kept this guy from going to jail, we've got him $500,000 for his accident, got hit by a shopping cart. I mean, we've, you know, done great things for this person, and we have bent over backwards doing them. And now, this person should reciprocate by a referring. So, there's often in offices an attitude of entitlement that gets in the way of being very organized and very proactive about getting referrals from the client or from the patient. They're supposed to happen. And they do. And so, there's also a sort of complacency of, gee, we're getting referrals. Usually, when I ask a practice owner, how are you doing with referrals? Almost everybody actually says good. Hardly anybody says bad. Most will answer, well, I think we do pretty good. Now, if you then start to probe, they really don't know what pretty good is, so usually they don't know the number. And at best, they'll know how many did we get last month. They won't know, but they've got a staff person who knows. So, they can go get that number. All right. But if you start to ask them true, measured efficacy questions, the chief one being how many referrals per active patient, client, or customer did you average last year? That number, they don't know. And it really has occurred to them to measure that. So, most of them say to me, I'm doing pretty well. When you investigate, they're doing horrible. They have a small number of really enthusiastic customers, clients, or patients who give them a lot of referrals and a huge number who give them none. That's what usually is going on. And in terms of averaged out, we X number of people this year are customers and we got this many referrals. The number is horrendous compared to 100% and compared often to some other industries in which you would think the referral rate would be a lot less. So, for example, the average chiropractor, the last time I had data, which isn't all that long ago, the last chiropractor gets one fourth of the number of referrals has the average used car sales person working at a dealership. Now, you would think you're a doc. You are laying on the hands. You are healing the sick. People are rising up and able to dance unless they're a Baptist and play the piano again. You would think that would motivate more referrals than the guy who beat his customer with a club, took his wallet out of his pocket and gave him a used car that now barely starts. I mean, it would just seem obvious. And yet, that's not what happens. So, thinking that it takes care of itself is really the first obstacle. The second big obstacle is that most patients and most customers and most clients aren't really capable of representing you, of telling your story. So, when we ask them to refer, they don't know what to do. They don't know how to do that. They don't really know what's expected of them. And so, since they don't know what to do and they don't know how to do it, they have no training in what it is they're supposed to do, they pretty much do nothing. Third reason. This never automatically necessarily comes up in their conversations with their circle of influence. Many of the kinds of practices that everybody has are not things that often, naturally, are a topic of conversation. But, if we haven't given them any other instruction and process for referring, at best, they will wait until it's caused by the conversation of someone else. And then, you hope they'll respond by talking about you. So, the next one is they feel awkward often. So, not everybody is happy talking about whatever. And, Rick, you don't mind if I use you, do you? So, in Rick's business, which are how many clinics across the country? 13. Okay, so 13 clinics that treat erectile dysfunction. Well, not everybody, even if it comes up in conversation, is not every 62-year-old guy is like eager to have that conversation. You'll find this amusing, by the way. I meant to tell you where. You know, Guthrie Rinker bought a penis pill company, a direct herbal thing. Yeah, so they bought an herbal. So, I go to the meeting, right, with the marketing team they have assigned to this. So, this is, you know, get an erection when you can't have an erection that stays erect. Pill, you know, herbal viagra thing. So, here's the marketing team that marches into the room, right? Five guys, no woman, right? All guys, right? The last I looked, the reason everybody wants the pill is the woman. It would be good to have her input. No, we got no women. We got all guys, right? The oldest, who's really the only DR guy, too, is 41. The rest of them are right out of school with their little college degrees in marketing, and they're like 30, right? They know their customer demo, though, which is probably the same as yours, right? 63, married for 15 years or longer to the same woman. Kids are out of the house. That's the customer demo, right? I said, so what's wrong with this picture? I said to Greg, I said, you gotta stay in. You got a team of people here working on this who get hard from a breeze. I mean, I have very little prowess anymore, but I have a long memory, you know? I mean, this is not a good thing. And here's the copy. You'll love the copy. You ready? Yes. Okay. Give your wife a night of rock star sex she'll never forget. This guy's 63. He's got a bad heart. And that's what he wants, and that's what she wants. He wants a rainbow. Square, tugly. Look real good. It stops. It comes to life. That's what he wants. I'm explaining these guys. They're not. So many times we are asking people to refer, what we are asking them to talk about is all for them to talk about. And it doesn't have to be this. It can be for the financial advisor who manages my money. Not everybody. Not everybody. And typically the older and more affluent is all that eager to have conversations about what they're doing with their money and who's doing it for them. So often what we don't think should be awkward is awkward for someone else. Often the patients have no self-benefiting cool story to tell. So by this I mean if you want a patient or a client or a customer to really be running around telling people about you, doing that has to make them look cool. It has to make them look smart. It has to elevate their status. Which by the way loops to celebrity, expert status and authority. Because that's the thing they are most likely to brag about. When this turned into cosmetic surgery. So that profession used to have the problem of women got work done but they went away to have the work done. And they told no one and they hid in a hotel till everything had healed and then they came back and they hoped that everybody would oh you look younger but not leap to the conclusion oh you look younger, must have been done with a knife. When this turned to the point that it is now where pretty much everybody wants to talk about it all the time amongst themselves. There's no stigma to it anymore at all. It turned on celebrity doctors. They figured out if they were celebrities people would brag about having the same doctor who does the Hollywood stars. Or who does the socialites in our town. And so in many cases we don't really give them any kind of story to tell that would raise their status in the eyes of the folks they hang out with. We don't give them a cool story. We are not a celebrity. We are not famous to any kind of audience. There is no real bragging right to knowing us or associating with us. And so they don't tell. So I don't think Chris D'Ary is here but Chris. Okay, water purifier business. Expensive water purifier. He has killed himself to make sure that the Kardashians have one. That Harrison Ford has one. There's eight or ten Hollywood celebrities who have this water purifier. And every customer now who has it now likes to tell people I got the same water purifier that the Kardashians got. That's an extremely valuable piece of currency. They bungle it. So they do go tell somebody. And so you will have always somebody who tells you, I'm telling everybody about you and you get no referrals from them. It's because of what they're telling them. They are telling them scary frightening things and telling the story badly to boot. If they're not trained professional communicators, God knows. First person to tell me about chiropractors kept me away from a chiropractor for three years. That's what he accomplished. So he's telling me I got a bad back. Here's what he's telling me. You got to go to my doc. My chiropractor, he'll fix you right up. At the time I knew nothing about chiropractors. So my question is, well, what goes on when you go? He says, well, he said, on this table. And it's this whizbang table and I'm on this table. And Doc starts working on my back and there's a foot pedal under the table. And at a certain point, when I least expected, he hits the foot pedal and he just about jumps on my back and the table falls out from under me and it cracks me into position and you can hear it snap, crack, and it will pop out. No way you're wrong. I said, what else? He says, well, then he comes around, he stands in front of me where my neck is and he fiddles around with my neck and he talks to me about the baseball game or something, so I'm distracted. And as soon as I'm distracted, he twists my head like this. And I mean, he really gets into it with his hips. And I mean, I could just hear it and feel it just crack. As if it cracked in half. And then he does it in the other direction and I feel great. I said, I'll run right down there. I mean, you know, driving past the chiropractors office, I crashed the lights because you don't want this guy running out there and getting you and dragging you out of the car. Now this person understand is perfectly well-intentioned and he is telling this story to everybody he knows. In a small town, this one guy could wipe out half of the marketplace for your practice in a matter of days, right? And more people will bungle it than they won't. Some people can't refer because they have very little stature and influence. There's not much you can do about that. They forget. Understandable they forget. It's not their job. So you get up every morning worrying about new customers, new patients, new clients. They don't get up worrying about your new customers, new patients, new clients. They got a top ten list for the day and it's easy to forget you. Maybe they think of you every once in a while on their own. They don't think it's important. They think you're buried. If you don't tell them, they think you're maxed out already. Especially if you structure your business in such a way that they only see you when you are maxed out. So if you come for a consulting day to me in my place, I have...this is a...this is a... it's kind of a side topic but it's worth mentioning. I have a list of knowns. I have a list of things I want you to know. You should have a list of things you want people to know about you. So one of the things I want people to know is I'm busy. I don't need you. And I don't need your money. So if you annoy me or if you appear too stupid, I'm just going to move your butt out of here. I want you to know I'm really, really busy. How can I make you know in my environment I'm really, really busy? Well, it can't be phones ringing and stuff because I don't allow that. And I don't have any people underfoot, so you can't see people running around with file folders like they're doing something. Because I don't have any of those either. I mean, the dog comes down and watches us, but this is not enough to make this point. So what you see is a lot of work. I mean, there's a space cleared for us and then there's project piles all over the place. And what most people do when I go to the bathroom is they roam around and look at all the project piles. Just like when you visit somebody's house, you look at a medicine cabinet and you look at the drawers. Come on, you all do, right? You all do it. I mean, every single one of you. You all do it, right? And so they roam around and they look at the project files and some of them speak and they take pictures of shit. They think they're getting away with something. So, however the adverse effect of this is, I've just shown them I'm up to my ear lobes of business. I'm barely squeezing them in. Now by schedule we had to figure out what I'm going to work on your project and now it'd be nice if that's a really nice client if they referred me another one. But what if I just set up a thought that, A, he doesn't need any clients, so why would I refer anybody and B, he's so busy, if I refer somebody I may get less attention and so I'm getting in my own way. So I'll keep my mouth shut about this, right? So they don't think it's important. They fear negative consequences. That patient who comes into your chiropractic practice only on Friday afternoons, usually in chiropractic on Friday, there's patients hanging from hooks. I mean they're everywhere, right? And that patient and the doc's running from room to room and the little red lights are going off outside the rooms to tell them which room to run to and he's clutching his heart and gasping for breath when he comes in to adjust you. Well that patient says, my God, if I refer one more patient in here, he's going to spend five minutes less with me and I'll kill over and drop that all together. So you don't see Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, when there's nobody in there, half the staff didn't even show up. They don't see that. So they fear negative consequences and they refer it and they weren't rewarded. The quickest way not to get a second referral is to do a really bad job of rewarding the first. People take it personally that they are not thanked and that they are not rewarded for good behavior. Some people are more annoyed by it than others, but it is adoring. So odds are, by the way, we'll do this whole meeting in this hotel with all of you here spending money and all these rooms and all this stuff and GKIC will not, if they get anything they'll get a little thank you note. They won't get any kind of a gift. They won't get anything. Go do a meeting at Disney, like Winners Weekend that only has 19 people in it. They thank you like you had brought the world there. This little gift bag, little pins, thank you letter personally when I get home, because they want you back and they want you to tell somebody. So these are all the reasons people don't refer, but when you combine a number of them, the biggest one is they really aren't trained, they aren't capable, they don't know how to do it, they don't have any good way to do it. We're asking them to do more than they are capable of doing and really then it's reasonable to ask them to do. So the best internal marketing systems take the task away from the patient or the customer or the client. So in some cases it's kind of easy to do it, in other cases it's not, but here's what we want to accomplish. We want to ask them to do something they can do. We want to ask them to do something they can do comfortably. We want to ask them to do something they'll get positive response for doing from those they do it to. Now this is important, don't miss this one. We want to ask them to do something that when they do it to their sister-in-law, their neighbor, their business peer in my case, that they get positive response for doing it. So in my world, in my professional practice, 8 out of 10 referrals, here's how they start. My client gives them, my good clients, give them a couple of my books. So however that conversation has occurred, Joe has said to Charlie, you know, you really should, this Kennedy guy is really the guy you should be talking to about this. Let me go over here, I've got a couple of his books on my shelf. Here's a couple of books. And so what the referred person says when they reach us is so and so gave me your books and I read your books. The cheapskate clients send them to Amazon to buy the books. Either way, the person has said to you, he's done something to you that you give him positive feedback for. Oh, thank you very much for the books. Thank you for telling me about the books. So that's important. They get brownie points for doing it and it's interesting to talk about. That's a very different approach because most strategy is go ye forth and tell somebody. So if we stop asking them to refer, now the question is what shall we ask them to do? If we're going to stop asking them to refer, what shall we ask them to do? We have what we call special report of the month. So I'll take you through it, but here's how it's used. We're now asking the patient, the customer, the client, to tell appropriate people in their world about the existence of the new available special report about X. So chiropractors, for example, are taught to use the Christmas card list, the prompt method, the same thing we teach in multi-level. So if any of you have had a decent sponsor in a multi-level company, one of the first things everybody does is the prompt list. Jay, who do you know who's always talking about needing extra money? Jay, who do you know who has a good attitude about selling? So the chiropractic version of that is Mary, who do you know does a lot of heavy lifting all the time on their job? Mary, who do you know who's always complaining about having headaches? And Mary has those people, but then again, here's where that ends. Well, Mary, go tell them about us. And Mary, I'm capable of doing that. But if we say to Mary, and everybody says it to her every time they see her this month, or a different version, we do it at a distance with email and mail and whatever, Mary, is there anybody in your life who's constantly complaining about headaches? And the doc asks the question, the staff person asks the question, there's a big sign in the lobby this month that asks that question, everybody's wearing buttons this month that ask that question, it's on the website, it's okay. Mary says, yeah, great, Mary, here's what we want you to do. And now we help Mary tell that person about the special report how never to have a headache again. They urge somebody to go somewhere now to get that report. So what they're usually urging them to do is what? If they refer, what are they telling them to do? What instruction do they give to the referee? Look them up, Google them, I think the website is this, or more commonly, call the office. For what? Scheduling an appointment. Big leap. Call them up, he can help you with your headaches. Schedule an appointment, go in there, get an exam. What's an exam like? Well, he puts you on a table, he hangs upside down. Uh-oh, this is not good. This is much less threatening and it's specific. Mary, you need to get this special report about never having headaches again from my doc. Here's how to get the report. Okay, I'll go get the report. So that's how we use this system. So the special report of the month system is about facilitating referrals. It can also be used to do direct lead generation in your own media. So my biggest gripe with customer newsletters, patient newsletters, is there's no lead generation offer in every issue. Well, why would we do lead generation? It's only going to people we already have as patients. Yeah, but they have family members, they have friends and neighbors and relatives, and we want them to tear this out like they would tear it out of the reader's digest or the newspaper and take it to somebody and say, you got headaches, you should get this free report. So the lead generation can be in your own media and again, drive referrals. So in health, for example, this is staying alive and well longer, how to reduce the risks of or avoid heart attack, stroke, dementia and Alzheimer's. Dentures versus implants, not just in other families, what secrets teens won't tell parents about, self-esteem schools, eat and lose weight. So these have great specificity and they cause people to tell people about these things. So when you promote information, instead of your products and services or practice, you immediately change the dynamic. First of all, everybody else is pitching products, services and the practice. That's what everybody else is pitching. You now are pitching something else, so you show up like no one else. Information is inherently more interesting than an advertising pitch. So in health, for example, the alternative health book and newsletter industry is a billion dollar a year business, as best anybody knows, because they're mostly privately held companies and there's a lot of small entrepreneurs like us, so it may be bigger, but it's at least a billion dollar a year industry. It sells predominantly by direct mail, but also by the web, direct to consumers information about health. How not to have heart attacks, how not to have strokes, how not to have backache, how to get a decent night's sleep, how not to have headaches, how to lose weight, keep it off, how to be more fit, how to get an erection, how to, okay, that's what it sells. If all you gotta do is copy it, if you're in the health environment, and the only thing you're changing is you're putting in your free information instead of the paid information. Brian Kurtz is going to speak at the Info Summit this year, who I work with a lot at Boardroom, Bottom Line Personal, Bottom Line Health, etc. They had this huge hit over the last three years selling the 30-day cure for diabetes book at three payments of $39.95 by direct mail, front end profitable, and I forget the exact number now, but it's in the hundreds of thousands of copies of books sold. Now, if I'm running a practice that can treat diabetic patients and help diabetic patients, my reasoning is if they can be in Connecticut and they can do direct mail to sell a $99 book with no back end, they don't sell pills, they don't refer anybody to a doctor and get a commission, they don't have any clinics, they don't, there's no back end to this except buy another book and sign up for the newsletter. If they can do this, surely I can do this for my practice if I can figure out how to mail the same people they mail to in my area. If I'm clean with the clinic, I'm all over this. They're not, because they don't get it, you get it. Same thing now in finance. So if you take the other big category of professional practices, they have something to do with money. Well, the alternative investment newsletter industry is also a billion-dollar-a-year industry. And they get people to buy books and newsletters about stocks, bonds, options, emu ranches, every known way that you can invest your money, okay, conservative, exotic, it's all out there. And if they can afford to do it just to be in the newsletter business and you have an average client value of $5,000, $8,000, $10,000, $12,000, has a financial advisor, a broker, and surely you can afford. So information is inherently more interesting. Proof is they pay for this. They're willing to pay for information. They're not willing to pay for brochures and marketing pitches and so forth. So providing information creates trust. You automatically again elevate your status in the eyes of the person, if you're the person who's providing information. From the consumer's guide to X, to the newsletter about, you automatically bring yourself up. And if you add all the pizzazz to it, Nick presented to you last night, you even go up a few more not-notches. You can position yourself sort of as a consumer advocate when you present information. You can certainly be the most interesting person in the room because when you provide stuff that people are eager to repeat, even if it's not directly relevant to what you sell, but it's in the subject matter of what it is that you sell, you enable, you're interesting and you enable other people to be interesting. So most of the people that subscribe to financial newsletters, what do they do? They glean fascinating facts that they then go and tell their buddies. Hey, Harry Dent said X. So-and-so said Y. Stansberry's predicting Z. Same thing with health. Hey, did you know there's a new study out that if you drink three cups of coffee a day, it cures diabetes. If you drink four, you'll be dead by Tuesday. Really? Where did you read that? I read that and so-and-so. So this is what goes on. So why wouldn't you want to be in that game? You also get to use one of the most powerful persuasion methods there is, which is demonstration, because you are demonstrating how smart you are about what it is that you do by the way you dispense the information. Skip a couple things. So specificity makes it easier for patients to refer. So we move when we use this strategy from if you know anybody who-and it's usually-it's that's usually said vague, right? If you know anybody who's a nice person just like you who you think we could help, please tell them about our practice. So the who is vague and the what to do is vague. This brings specificity to the process. Headaches. Next month it's chronic back pain. Next month it's a-it's feet that hurt. Next month-so if you know-it's specific for them and hears how to tell them to get it. So the more specificity you put into the process, the better the results. So here's what a monthly promotion checklist actually looks like. Right? We need a free report that is specific. So in finance it might-this month it might be the 401k tax bomb for anybody who has more than $200,000 tied up in a 401k or pension plan. Next month the financial visors report is more eloquently titled than this but it is for grandparents who are concerned about protecting assets for grandchildren without the children in between who are getting divorced and are fighting with each other and are completely unreliable and take drugs being able to get at the money. How do I do that? So specificity of the free report every month. Now depending upon the size of your-you may be able to reuse some but there's lead generation ad copy for the free report. The lead generation ads in your own media. There's a get this thing for a friend form stuck in your own media so they can give the form to somebody or they can fill it out form and have it sent to them. There's free report ad. The lead generation ad turned into a good size card but one that will fit in a pocket or purse so every patient gets three or four of those so they can hand out the cards and it drives people to the website or the phone in order to get the report. There's a lead generation postcard to the house list, lead generation email to the house list, in-office signage, weekly email with amazing facts that also ties to the free report, a landing page and a capture site tied to the free report and then optional there's some additional stuff. And obviously you can mix up the media. They don't have to all be reports. It could be your report this month, an audio CD next month, a DVD the month after that, an online video the month after that you can mix the media. That's really, that's what this system looks like when it is implemented. So I'll quickly show you, here's a lead generation ad. Finally sleep well every night, feel ten years younger every day, new free special report for you if you don't sleep well your partner sleeps poorly or you have a family member friend who complains of trouble sleep, daytime tiredness or a partner who snores, tosses the turn to keep them awake. The new report how to get a good night's sleep every night is now available. This report reveals the causes of interrupted and unrestful sleep, causes of health risks including heart attack, stroke and dementia of snoring and proven ways to improve sleep with diet exercise. This copy is written as if we were selling this information but we are going to give it away. Ultimately get this report for yourself, your spouse or a friend at www.whatsis or by calling whatsis or email your name to Cindy at whdettlesenner.com and we need to name in the street address of the person to send the reports to. That's the lead generation ad for the report. That's on the back of a card so everybody can have cards in their pockets. That's used in the email, that's used in the newsletter, that's used everywhere. Next month we put all that away and we trot out the next one. A month after that we put all that away and we trot out the next one. By the way it automatically also makes you more interesting to a regular clientele or patient base because you are appearing with new specific information month after month after month. So a comprehensive internal marketing system has that plus a bunch of stuff wrapped around it. This is the antithesis of hey would you please tell your friends and neighbors about us. One tool that helps a lot and I'm going to show you two made by Parteeve. Did you get a mic? Okay I may want to ask you a question. So shock and awe packages. Many of you are now familiar with the shock and awe package. The role they have in referring is the patient who comes in through this kind of device feels better about you, clientele tells other people about you. But these are also the at some point hey you have a great experience here. At some point you're going to tell somebody about us. We usually put them in plastic bags so here's three of our, there's a book, oh there's the thing in here. So here's three of our packages just like the one you got. I put each one in a plastic bag so you can put it in a trunk of your car if you want to and it won't get grease all over it. And at any opportunity you have when you have a conversation with somebody about it, just here's my doctor's information kit and his video book in this case. Or here's my doctor's information kit and my book. He's a doctor we trust with our healthcare. I think you'll find this really interesting. That's all you have to say. You just have to say here's information from my doctor including his book. I think you'll find it really interesting. He's the guy we trust with our healthcare. That's all you have to say Mary. Okay you do it three times so Mary learns the script. Alright so now that's kind of implanted. We've given her a good tool to give away. If it's got a book or a video book in it, it gives her help. It gives her browning points for doing it. So this is your shocking off for Dr. Weisch. Do you want to tell me anything about it? You've got 30 seconds. Yes sir. It follows everything you said. I mind mapped it and implemented it. And this is the video book that's in it. Yes. Which when you open it up, it plays right? Or do you push a button? Yes sir. No it just plays. It'll automatically come on. Which it's not automatically come on. It's not. Hey, it's not coming on. Give me that one. It's always the copier salesman's bane of existence. It's demonstration. Take my word for it. It's supposed to. It's supposed to do this. There we go. Video loading at least appears on the screen. And then the little video comes on in the book. So this package when it works, and then this is Dr. Burleson's, who's going to come here in a second. This is Dr. Burleson's. The format is largely the same. It's built on a template literally that I created. Slight variances obviously. This is does your child be braces? This is not a braces practice. How to get the radiant smile. So the product is different. It's important that only one piece of this whole thing has to do with the product by the way. And so you build these four people, correct? Yes sir. Yes sir. It has a quiz and everything. You've ripped off my format. You call me a parasite and I'm just living up to your. There you go. And you. And he's doing it brilliantly by the way. For clients in all kinds of fields. So these tools, if you want people to refer you got to give them good tools. They have to have a bag of tools. You can't give them. I mean you can. I saw Dave do this. Magnetic marketing thing I cringed. It's better than nothing. But you know, giving them three business cards has all the problems. See this relieves these problems. This actually motivate. So we've done this with financial visors here consistently is what has happened. The number of referrals has gone up. The speed of referral from the client has improved. And about 10% are back within one to two weeks wanting more. I gave those three you gave me out. I gave them out. Is it okay? Could I have some more? No. We only let a client have three a year. So the fear is always erroneous. The other thing it does, it positions us as the big case lawyers. Dustin. I would echo that. I think your question is, can you point to one thing that affects your no show rate? And I think it's the wrong question to ask. I think you need to look at a million things Ben does and I do to systematically put people in the position where they would swim through a lake of hungry alligators just to get to Ben's door to please take my case. That's what all this does. So have we gone back and measured our close rate on people who have gone through the package? Yes, we closed 90% of our cases. If they haven't read it systematically, we kind of pushed them out. So if you, see if you have a significant no show rate in any business, right, there's, you really don't have a no show problem. Right. You have a source problem. Right. So you are feeding bad customers into your system. Right. Or you have a motivational problem in that a they feel they cannot show and reschedule and it's no big deal. They feel you're an interchangeable commodity and they just discovered somebody six miles closer and what the hell I'll go over there. Right. Those are really your two problems. You fix those two things. The no show rate goes away in just about every business. And by the way, all sorts of businesses have it. People that run preview seminars have a no show rate of people not showing up at the preview seminars. Doctors have it. Lawyers have it. CPAs have it. Jim's that sell by appointment have it. And so it really is actually the problem is even erroneously defined. Right. The problem is do it source and the mindset you've created about that. You know, I mean, so like people in my world who are actually actively seeking new clients will complain about people scheduling phone appointments with them, you know, and not showing up. I can't recall the last time that's happened to me. Right. Because the positioning is right. The terror factor of they'll never speak to me again, which if that did happen, it's actually what would happen. I mean, it just doesn't happen. So it's really you really have to define the problem right before you try and solve it. Yes, sir. Dr. Burrelson, you mentioned a website that you emulated or kind of copied for your shotgun off. What was the plane? The plane truth about braces dot com. So that's what you use. D23. D23. Yes. Okay. And then Ben, just a real quick question. If it's if it's it's always this piece and the ROI here competing against this piece in the ROI here and I'll do as many of those pieces as I can. It's not the answer that you wanted. It's not a there are no simple answers to complex questions. Having said that, if you look around what all the other lawyers in your community are doing, you only have to be smarter than the next least smart guy and more willing. I mean, that's where I would start if I'm you. Yeah, my answer has always been as much as you can spend. This would be plated in solid gold if it's what it took to get the result that we get. And so I think last quarter we gave enough enough iPads away that Apple called us. We were buying more iPads than our local best buy and they blacklisted my office. We had to go buy them in cash. So these these types of things literally are all about the result they get. And so design your business so you can spend as much as you can, at least more than your competitor. Does that make sense? Yeah. Look, the last thing I'll say about it is is this one of the things that everybody ought to know, because all kind of budgeting questions come up. All of the stuff that's taught in textbooks and that your CPA is going to give you on spreadsheets and all that stuff. It doesn't apply to us. We're in direct response and we're in direct marketing. We have a separate set of math and statistics that we use to manage money than all other kinds of businesses. So if you don't have a CPA who does direct marketing and direct response companies, that's OK as long as you know what you're doing. But getting advice from him about that is useless because he is always going to well your ad budget should be x percentage of your growth. It doesn't apply. If you want kind of a textbook on this Bob Stone's book, Direct Marketing, it has the math in it that the direct marketing industry uses. Gentlemen, thank you, thank you, thank you, thank you. Please thank them. If I... And I don't want to take those books home so I'll just meet you on the side if you, you know, give them... Ben is giving away his stuff. His samples. All right. Dustin is not. I am not. Thank you all. I hope you had a good session and you are on a break. Ladies and gentlemen, we are taking a 20 minute break. 20 minutes. Thanks.
